---
title: "Biostat 203B Homework 2"
subtitle: Due Feb 8, 2026 @ 11:59PM
author: "CHIH WEI HSU, UID: 706788612"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
    number-sections: false
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: false
    link-external-icon: true
    link-external-newwindow: true
---

Display machine information for reproducibility:
```{r}
sessionInfo()
```

Load necessary libraries (you can add more as needed).
```{r setup}
library(arrow)
library(data.table)
library(duckdb)
library(memuse)
library(pryr)
library(R.utils)
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
```

Display memory information of your computer
```{r}
memuse::Sys.meminfo()
```

In this exercise, we explore various tools for ingesting the [MIMIC-IV](https://physionet.org/content/mimiciv/3.1/) data introduced in [homework 1](https://ucla-biostat-203b.github.io/2026winter/hw/hw1/hw1.html).

Display the contents of MIMIC `hosp` and `icu` data folders:

```{bash}
ls -l ~/mimic/hosp
```

```{bash}
ls -l ~/mimic/icu
```

## Q1. `read.csv` (base R) vs `read_csv` (tidyverse) vs `fread` (data.table)

### Q1.1 Speed, memory, and data types

There are quite a few utilities in R for reading plain text data files. Let us test the speed of reading a moderate sized compressed csv file, `admissions.csv.gz`, by three functions: `read.csv` in base R, `read_csv` in tidyverse, and `fread` in the data.table package.

Which function is fastest? 

Base R
```{r}
r_baseline_time <- system.time({
  r_admissions <- read.csv("~/mimic/hosp/admissions.csv.gz")
})
r_baseline_time
```

Tidyverse
```{r}
t_baseline_time <- system.time({
  t_admissions <- read_csv(
    "~/mimic/hosp/admissions.csv.gz",
    show_col_types = FALSE
    )
})
t_baseline_time
```

Data.table
```{r}
f_baseline_time <- system.time({
  f_admissions <- fread("~/mimic/hosp/admissions.csv.gz")
})
f_baseline_time
```
**solutins**: The fastest function for reading the `admissions.csv.gz` file is `fread` from the data.table package, followed by `read_csv` from tidyverse, and then `read.csv` from base R. 

Is there difference in the (default) parsed data types? 
```{r}
str(r_admissions)
str(t_admissions)
str(f_admissions)
```
**solution:** Yes, there are differences in the default parsed data types among the three functions.

How much memory does each resultant dataframe or tibble use? (Hint: `system.time` measures run times; `pryr::object_size` measures memory usage; all these readers can take gz file as input without explicit decompression.)
```{r}
object_size(r_admissions)
object_size(t_admissions)
object_size(f_admissions)
```
**solution:** The memory usage of the resultant dataframes or tibbles varies among the three functions. Generally, `fread` from the data.table package tends to use less memory compared to `read.csv` from base R and `read_csv` from tidyverse, which can be attributed to its optimized parsing and memory management.

### Q1.2 User-supplied data types

Re-ingest `admissions.csv.gz` by indicating appropriate column data types in `read_csv`. 
```{bash}
zcat < ~/mimic/hosp/admissions.csv.gz | 
head -2 | 
tr ',' '\n' |
nl
```
```{r}
ct <- cols(
  subject_id = col_integer(),
  hadm_id = col_integer(),
  admittime = col_datetime(format = ""),
  dischtime = col_datetime(format = ""),
  deathtime = col_datetime(format = ""),
  admission_type = col_factor(),
  admit_provider_id = col_factor(),
  admission_location = col_factor(),
  discharge_location = col_factor(),
  insurance = col_factor(),
  language = col_factor(),
  marital_status = col_factor(),
  race = col_factor(),
  edregtime = col_datetime(format = ""),
  edouttime = col_datetime(format = ""),
  hospital_expire_flag = col_integer()
)
t_typed_time <- system.time({
  t_admissions_typed <- read_csv(
    "~/mimic/hosp/admissions.csv.gz",
    col_types = ct,
    show_col_types = FALSE
  )
})
t_typed_time
```
Does the run time change?
```{r}
cat("baseline_time: ",t_baseline_time["elapsed"], "\n", "typed_time: ", t_typed_time["elapsed"])
```
**solution:** The run time after indicating appropriate column data types in `read_csv` is reduced compared to the default behavior. This is because when you specify the column data types, `read_csv` can optimize the parsing process and avoid the overhead of guessing the data types for each column, which can be time-consuming for large files.

How much memory does the result tibble use? (Hint: `col_types` argument in `read_csv`; result tibble should be less than 50MB.)
```{r}
object_size(t_admissions_typed)
```




## Q2. Ingest big data files

<p align="center">
  <img src="./bigfile.png" width="50%">
</p>

Let us focus on a bigger file, `labevents.csv.gz`, which is about 130x bigger than `admissions.csv.gz`.
```{bash}
ls -l ~/mimic/hosp/labevents.csv.gz
```
Display the first 10 lines of this file.
```{bash}
zcat < ~/mimic/hosp/labevents.csv.gz | 
head -10
```

### Q2.1 Ingest `labevents.csv.gz` by `read_csv`

<p align="center">
  <img src="./readr_logo.png" width="20%">
</p>


Try to ingest `labevents.csv.gz` using `read_csv`. What happens? If it takes more than 3 minutes on your computer, then abort the program and report your findings. 

```{r,eval: false}
t_labevents <- read_csv(
  "~/mimic/hosp/labevents.csv.gz", 
  show_col_types = FALSE
)
```

**solution:** The program takes more than 3 minutes and eventually runs out of memory. It is because `labevents.csv.gz` is a big data file (about 130GB uncompressed) that cannot fit into memory. The default behavior of `read_csv` is to read the entire file into memory, which causes the program to run out of memory and fail.


### Q2.2 Ingest selected columns of `labevents.csv.gz` by `read_csv`

Try to ingest only columns `subject_id`, `itemid`, `charttime`, and `valuenum` in `labevents.csv.gz` using `read_csv`.  Does this solve the ingestion issue? (Hint: `col_select` argument in `read_csv`.)

```{r,eval: true}
t_labevents_sel <- read_csv(
  "~/mimic/hosp/labevents.csv.gz",
  show_col_types = FALSE, 
  col_select = c(subject_id, itemid, charttime, valuenum)
)
t_labevents_sel
```

**solution:** Yes, it solves the ingestion issue. By selecting only the necessary columns, we reduce the amount of data that needs to be read into memory, which allows `read_csv` to successfully ingest the file without running out of memory. However, it may still take a long time to read the file due to its large size.

### Q2.3 Ingest a subset of `labevents.csv.gz`

<p align="center">
  <img src="./linux_logo.png" width="20%">
</p>

Our first strategy to handle this big data file is to make a subset of the `labevents` data.  Read the [MIMIC documentation](https://mimic.mit.edu/docs/iv/modules/hosp/labevents/) for the content in data file `labevents.csv`.

In later exercises, we will only be interested in the following lab items: albumin (50862), creatinine (50912), potassium (50971), sodium (50983), chloride (50902), bicarbonate (50882), hematocrit (51221), white blood cell count (51301), and glucose (50931) and the following columns: `subject_id`, `itemid`, `charttime`, `valuenum`. Write a Bash command to extract these columns and rows from `labevents.csv.gz` and save the result to a new file `labevents_filtered.csv.gz` in the current working directory. (Hint: Use `zcat <` to pipe the output of `labevents.csv.gz` to `awk` and then to `gzip` to compress the output. Do **not** put `labevents_filtered.csv.gz` in Git! To save render time, you can put `#| eval: false` at the beginning of this code chunk. TA will change it to `#| eval: true` before rendering your qmd file.)

```{bash}
#| eval: false
ITEMS='50862 50912 50971 50983 50902 50882 51221 51301 50931'

zcat < ~/mimic/hosp/labevents.csv.gz |
awk -F',' -v items="$ITEMS" '
BEGIN {
  OFS=",";
  n=split(items,a," ");
  for (i=1; i<=n; i++) keep[a[i]]=1;
}
NR==1 { print "subject_id","itemid","charttime","valuenum"; next }
keep[$5] { print $2,$5,$7,$10 }
' |
gzip > labevents_filtered.csv.gz
```

Display the first 10 lines of the new file `labevents_filtered.csv.gz`. 

```{bash}
zcat < labevents_filtered.csv.gz | head -10
```
How many lines are in this new file, excluding the header? 
```{bash}
zcat < labevents_filtered.csv.gz |
tail -n +2 |
wc -l
```

How long does it take `read_csv` to ingest `labevents_filtered.csv.gz`?
```{r}
system.time({
  t_labevents_filt <- read_csv(
    "labevents_filtered.csv.gz"
  )
})
```

### Q2.4 Ingest `labevents.csv` by Apache Arrow

<p align="center">
  <img src="./arrow_logo.png" width="30%">
</p>

Our second strategy is to use [Apache Arrow](https://arrow.apache.org/) for larger-than-memory data analytics. Unfortunately Arrow does not work with gz files directly. 

First decompress `labevents.csv.gz` to `labevents.csv` and put it in the current working directory (do not add it in Git!). To save render time, put `#| eval: false` at the beginning of this code chunk. TA will change it to `#| eval: true` when rendering your qmd file.
```{bash}
#| eval: false
gzip -d -c ~/mimic/hosp/labevents.csv.gz > labevents.csv
```

Then use [`arrow::open_dataset`](https://arrow.apache.org/docs/r/reference/open_dataset.html) to ingest `labevents.csv`, select columns, and filter `itemid` as in Q2.3. How long does the ingest+select+filter process take? 
```{r}
items <- c(
  50862, 50912, 50971, 50983, 50902,
  50882, 51221, 51301, 50931
)
system.time({
t_labevents_arrow <-
  open_dataset("./labevents.csv", format = "csv") |>
  select(subject_id, itemid, charttime, valuenum) |>
  filter(itemid %in% items) |>
  collect()
})
```

Display the number of rows and the first 10 rows of the result tibble, and make sure they match those in Q2.3. (Hint: use `dplyr` verbs for selecting columns and filtering rows.)

```{r}
nrow(t_labevents_arrow)
head(t_labevents_arrow, 10)
```

Write a few sentences to explain what Apache Arrow is. Imagine you want to explain it to a layman in an elevator.

**solution:** Apache Arrow is a cross-language development platform for in-memory data. It provides a standardized way to represent and process data in memory, which allows for efficient data sharing and interoperability between different programming languages and systems. In simple terms, it is like a common language that different software can use to talk to each other when working with data, making it faster and easier to analyze large datasets without having to worry about compatibility issues.

### Q2.5 Compress `labevents.csv` to Parquet format and ingest/select/filter

<p align="center">
  <img src="./parquet_logo.png" width="30%">
</p>

Re-write the csv file `labevents.csv` in the binary Parquet format (Hint: [`arrow::write_dataset`](https://arrow.apache.org/docs/r/reference/write_dataset.html).)
```{r}
write_dataset(
  open_dataset("./labevents.csv", format = "csv"),
  path = "labevents_parquet",
  format = "parquet"
)
```

How large is the Parquet file(s)? 
```{bash}
ls -l labevents_parquet
```
**solution:** The Parquet file(s) is about 2.74GB.

How long does the ingest+select+filter process of the Parquet file(s) take? 
```{r}
system.time({
t_labevents_parquet <-
  open_dataset("./labevents_parquet", format = "parquet") |>
  select(subject_id, itemid, charttime, valuenum) |>
  filter(itemid %in% items) |>
  collect()
})
```

Display the number of rows and the first 10 rows of the result tibble and make sure they match those in Q2.3. (Hint: use `dplyr` verbs for selecting columns and filtering rows.)
```{r}
nrow(t_labevents_parquet)
head(t_labevents_parquet, 10)
```
Write a few sentences to explain what the Parquet format is. Imagine you want to explain it to a layman in an elevator.
**solution:** Parquet is a columnar storage file format that is optimized for big data processing. It is designed to efficiently store and query large datasets by organizing data in a way that allows for faster read and write operations. In simple terms, it is like a more efficient way to store data on disk, which can significantly reduce the time it takes to read and analyze large datasets compared to traditional row-based formats like CSV.

### Q2.6 DuckDB

<p align="center">
  <img src="./duckdb_logo.png" width="20%">
</p>

Ingest the Parquet file, convert it to a DuckDB table by [`arrow::to_duckdb`](https://arrow.apache.org/docs/r/reference/to_duckdb.html), select columns, and filter rows as in Q2.5. How long does the ingest+convert+select+filter process take? 
```{r}
system.time({
t_labevents_parquet |>
  select(subject_id, itemid, charttime, valuenum) |>
  filter(itemid %in% items) |>
  to_duckdb() |>
  collect() -> t_labevents_duckdb
})
```
Display the number of rows and the first 10 rows of the result tibble and make sure they match those in Q2.3. (Hint: use `dplyr` verbs for selecting columns and filtering rows.)
```{r}
nrow(t_labevents_duckdb)
head(t_labevents_duckdb, 10)
```
Write a few sentences to explain what DuckDB is. Imagine you want to explain it to a layman in an elevator.
**solution:** DuckDB is an in-process SQL database management system that is designed for analytical workloads. It allows you to run SQL queries on large datasets without the need for a separate database server, making it easy to integrate into applications and workflows. In simple terms, it is like having a powerful SQL engine built into your application that can efficiently query and analyze large datasets without the overhead of setting up and managing a traditional database server.

## Q3. Ingest and filter `chartevents.csv.gz`

[`chartevents.csv.gz`](https://mimic.mit.edu/docs/iv/modules/icu/chartevents/) contains all the charted data available for a patient. During their ICU stay, the primary repository of a patient’s information is their electronic chart. The `itemid` variable indicates a single measurement type in the database. The `value` variable is the value measured for `itemid`. The first 10 lines of `chartevents.csv.gz` are
```{bash}
zcat < ~/mimic/icu/chartevents.csv.gz | head -10
```
How many rows? 433 millions.
```{bash}
#| eval: false
zcat < ~/mimic/icu/chartevents.csv.gz | 
tail -n +2 | 
wc -l
```
[`d_items.csv.gz`](https://mimic.mit.edu/docs/iv/modules/icu/d_items/) is the dictionary for the `itemid` in `chartevents.csv.gz`.
```{bash}
zcat < ~/mimic/icu/d_items.csv.gz | head -10
```
In later exercises, we are interested in the vitals for ICU patients: heart rate (220045), mean non-invasive blood pressure (220181), systolic non-invasive blood pressure (220179), body temperature in Fahrenheit (223761), and respiratory rate (220210). Retrieve a subset of `chartevents.csv.gz` only containing these items, using the favorite method you learnt in Q2. 

### Q3.1 Ingest `chartevents.csv.gz` by Apache Arrow 

I first Decompress `chartevents.csv.gz` to `chartevents.csv`.
```{bash}
#| eval: false
gzip -d -c ~/mimic/icu/chartevents.csv.gz > chartevents.csv
```

Ingest+select+filter process.
```{r}
icu_items <- c(
  220405, 220181, 220179, 223761, 220210
)

t_chartevents_arrow <-
  open_dataset("./chartevents.csv", format = "csv") |>
  select(subject_id, itemid, value, valuenum) |>
  filter(itemid %in% icu_items) |>
  collect()
```

Read d_itemid.csv.gz
```{r}
d_itemid <-
  read_csv(
    "~/mimic/icu/d_items.csv.gz",
    col_select = c(itemid, label),
    show_col_types = FALSE
  )
```
Left join with d_items to get itemid's label.
```{r}
t_chartevents_labeled <-
  t_chartevents_arrow |>
  left_join(d_itemid, by = "itemid")
```
Display the number of rows and the first 10 rows of the result tibble.
```{r}
nrow(t_chartevents_labeled)
head(t_chartevents_labeled, 10)
```
## Q4. AI assistant

Which AI assistants (e.g., GitHub Copilot) do you use when working on this assignment? Which AI model (e.g., GPT-5 mini, GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4.5) does the AI assistant use? How do you use them? Do you think they help improve your productivity? 

Give 5 instances where AI model gave incorrect or misleading answers. You can use screenshots or copy-paste the Q&A.
